title: Return Types
description: Get answers back from your functions.

slides.title: Two-Way Streets 🛣️
slides.content:
## Getting Answers

So far, our functions just *do* something (like `println`). But often, we want a function to calculate a value and **give it back** to us so we can use it.

slides.title: The Return Type 🏷️
slides.content:
To return a value, we must declare the **Return Type** at the end of the function definition, after a colon `:`.

Then, we use the `return` keyword to actually send the data back out.

quizData.question: What happens when the program executes the `return` keyword inside a function?

options.text: It goes back to the top of the function and restarts.
It sends the value back and immediately EXITS the function.
It prints the value to the screen.

quizData.explanation: Return operates exactly like `break` in a loop. It sends the value and instantly exits the function. Any code written beneath `return` will never run.

slides.title: The Unit Type 📦
slides.content:
## What if returning nothing?

If a function just prints something and doesn't return any data, its hidden return type is called `Unit`.

You don't have to write `: Unit` because Kotlin automatically assumes it for you.

quizData.question: If you define `fun greet(): String { println("Hi") }`, what error occurs?

options.text: You promised to return a String, but didn't write a `return` statement.
You cannot print inside a String function.

quizData.explanation: If you declare a specific return type like `: String` or `: Int`, you are contractually obligated to use the `return` keyword, otherwise the code won't compile.

slides.title: Single-Expression Functions ✨
slides.content:
If your function is literally just one single line of math or logic, Kotlin lets you drop the curly braces `{}` and the `return` keyword!

Use the `=` symbol instead. It looks incredibly clean.
